summary.title: StudentHub Threat Model
summary.description: STRIDE-based threat model for a hypothetical student web portal

threats.title: Email injection or phishing via outbound mail
threats.description: If user-controlled strings become email headers or HTML bodies without sanitization, attackers can inject SMTP headers or malicious links sent as official StudentHub mail.
threats.mitigation: Use a transactional email API with template escaping; fixed From domain with SPF DKIM DMARC; strip CRLF from inputs; rate limit outbound mail per user.

threats.title: credential stuffing/ brute force
threats.description: Usernames and passwords may be exposed to network observers if encryption is misconfigured, or to client-side malware via phishing and fake login pages that mimic StudentHub.
threats.mitigation: Strict HTTPS-only with HSTS; phishing-resistant MFA where possible; clear branding and canonical hostnames; security awareness; detect and block lookalike domains.

threats.title: Token forgery if signing keys are compromised
threats.description: If token signing keys are static, broadly shared, or stored in source control, attackers who obtain the key can mint valid tokens.
threats.mitigation: Store keys in a managed secret vault; automate key rotation; separate signing keys per environment; monitor for anomalous token issuance

threats.title: Metadata leakage in auth service calls
threats.description: Auth requests may carry usernames, device fingerprints, or tenant identifiers. Logs or traces could expose this data to unauthorized operators.
threats.mitigation: Minimize PII in logs; field-level redaction; access-controlled observability; retention limits aligned with GDPR-style expectations for student data

threats.title: New STRIDE threat
threats.description: Provide a description for this threat
threats.mitigation: Provide remediation for this threat or a reason if status is N/A

threats.title: Reflected or stored XSS in HTML responses
threats.description: The web application returns HTML that may embed untrusted data (errors, names, search results). If output is not encoded, an attacker can inject script executed in the student browser, stealing session tokens or performing actions as the victim.
threats.mitigation: Use a template engine with contextual output encoding; adopt a strict Content-Security-Policy; sanitize and validate all dynamic fields; avoid concatenating user input into HTML strings.

threats.title: Sensitive data leakage via HTML or headers
threats.description: Responses may leak internal identifiers, stack traces, or session identifiers in URLs or error pages, helping attackers refine follow-on attacks.
threats.mitigation: Centralized safe error pages; never expose stack traces to users; use HttpOnly Secure SameSite cookies for session tokens; redact internal details from client-visible output.

threats.title: Audit log tampering or selective deletion
threats.description: If audit logs are writable by the same compromised principal that performed the abuse, attackers can erase evidence of admin misuse.
threats.mitigation: Append-only log storage; separate credentials for log writers vs readers; ship logs to immutable WORM or SIEM; integrity hashing of log chains.

threats.title: Session store breach exposes all active users
threats.description: Central session storage is a concentration risk. A single breach could leak thousands of active sessions at once.
threats.mitigation: Encrypt session payloads at rest; network isolation; least-privilege access; intrusion detection on session store; short session TTL.

threats.title: Malicious file upload (malware polyglot)
threats.description: Students upload files that may contain malware or double extensions. If the server stores and later serves files unsafely, other users or admins could be compromised when opening submissions.
threats.mitigation: Virus scanning; file type sniffing; strip macros; store outside web root; serve with `Content-Disposition: attachment`; randomize filenames; sandbox preview generation.

threats.title: Upload denial-of-service
threats.description: Attackers upload extremely large or numerous files to exhaust disk, CPU, or connection limits, blocking legitimate submissions before deadlines.
threats.mitigation: Per-user quotas, maximum file size, rate limits, asynchronous processing with backpressure, storage alerts, and autoscaling caps.

threats.title: Unauthorized modification of student submissions
threats.description: Admins can legitimately manage coursework, but a hijacked admin session could alter grades-linked artifacts or delete evidence of misconduct.
threats.mitigation: Immutable submission versioning; separate read vs write roles; time-limited admin edit windows; student notification on post-deadline admin changes.

threats.title: Academic integrity violations via data access
threats.description: Admins viewing live submissions before deadlines could leak answers to other students if access is not time-gated.
threats.mitigation: Blind grading mode until deadline; access logs per file; policy-based access windows tied to assessment rules.

threats.title: SQL injection on coursework queries
threats.description: Dynamic SQL built from search filters, filenames, or user IDs can allow attackers to read or modify arbitrary rows in the coursework store.
threats.mitigation: Parameterized queries; strict ORM usage; principle of least privilege on DB accounts; WAF rules for SQLi patterns; code review for all DB access paths.

threats.title: Unauthorized access to other students’ submissions
threats.description: Missing object-level authorization checks could let one student list or download another student’s uploaded files by guessing identifiers.
threats.mitigation: Enforce ownership checks on every read/write; use non-guessable object IDs; signed download URLs with short TTL; audit all access to submission blobs

threats.title: Session store breach exposes all active users
threats.description: Central session storage is a concentration risk. A single breach could leak thousands of active sessions at once.
threats.mitigation: Encrypt session payloads at rest; network isolation; least-privilege access; intrusion detection on session store; short session TTL.

threats.title: SQL injection in credential lookup
threats.description: If the auth service builds SQL from unsanitized input, attackers can alter queries to bypass password checks or dump the user table
threats.mitigation: Parameterized queries only; least-privilege DB role for auth; input validation; static and dynamic analysis in CI; regular penetration testing on auth endpoints.

threats.title: Mass user record exfiltration
threats.description: A compromised auth query path or over-privileged DB account could export all student credentials or profile attributes in one operation.
threats.mitigation: Column-level access where possible; rate limits on bulk reads; anomaly detection on large result sets; encryption at rest for sensitive columns.

threats.title: Mass account tampering via admin queries
threats.description: A compromised admin panel session could bulk-update roles, reset passwords, or mark accounts suspended without business approval.
threats.mitigation: Transaction limits, approval workflows for bulk changes, soft-delete with restore window, and database triggers logging critical column changes.

threats.title: PII exfiltration via admin reporting queries
threats.description: Admin list/search features may export entire user tables if export endpoints lack pagination and authorization checks.
threats.mitigation: Export quotas; watermark exports; role-scoped column visibility; DLP monitoring on download endpoints.

threats.title: Session data integrity not verified
threats.description: If session payloads are not signed or integrity-checked, a compromised web tier could forge session records to impersonate users.
threats.mitigation: Signed opaque tokens or server-side-only session references; HMAC or authenticated encryption on serialized session blobs; mutual TLS between app and session service.

threats.title: Unauthorized read of user records
threats.description: Data leaving the user store toward the auth service could be intercepted or logged excessively, exposing password hashes, roles, and flags such as `is_admin`
threats.mitigation: Encrypt data in transit between services; restrict DB credentials to SELECT on required columns; avoid logging full user rows; mask roles in non-admin logs.

threats.title: Stale or tampered user record used for authorization
threats.description: If cached user records are not invalidated after role changes, the auth service may grant access based on outdated `is_admin` or `is_seller` flags. |
threats.mitigation: Short TTL on cached identity; explicit invalidation on role change; version column or ETag on user rows; re-fetch on privilege-sensitive operations
